title: The Recipe Notebook
text_zh: 搬家的时候，我在一个旧箱子里找到了妈妈的菜谱本。那是一个普通的笔记本，封面已经发黄了，角上有油渍。翻开来看，里面是妈妈的字迹，一道一道菜写得很仔细。每道菜都写了需要什么材料、多少分量、怎么做，有些地方还画了简单的图。有的页面旁边写着小小的注释，比如「这个菜你爸爸特别喜欢吃」「小宝第一次吃就吃了两碗饭」。看到「小宝」两个字我笑了，那是我小时候的小名。菜谱里有些菜我吃过很多次，比如糖醋排骨和蒸鸡蛋羹；有些菜我从来没有印象，也许是妈妈学了但后来没有做过的。我拍了每一页的照片存在手机里，然后小心地把菜谱本放回箱子。这个本子不只是菜谱，它记录的是一个家庭的日常，是妈妈用心生活的证据。
text_pinyin: Bānjiā de shíhou, wǒ zài yí gè jiù xiāngzi lǐ zhǎo dào le māma de càipǔ běn. Nà shì yí gè pǔtōng de bǐjìběn, fēngmiàn yǐjīng fā huáng le, jiǎo shàng yǒu yóuzì. Fānkāi lái kàn, lǐmiàn shì māma de zìjì, yí dào yí dào cài xiě de hěn zǐxì. Měi dào cài dōu xiě le xūyào shénme cáiliào, duōshǎo fènliàng, zěnme zuò, yǒuxiē dìfāng hái huà le jiǎndān de tú. Yǒu de yèmiàn pángbiān xiě zhe xiǎo xiǎo de zhùshì, bǐrú「zhège cài nǐ bàba tèbié xǐhuan chī」「Xiǎo Bǎo dì yī cì chī jiù chī le liǎng wǎn fàn」. Kàn dào「Xiǎo Bǎo」liǎng gè zì wǒ xiào le, nà shì wǒ xiǎo shíhou de xiǎomíng. Càipǔ lǐ yǒuxiē cài wǒ chī guò hěn duō cì, bǐrú tángcù páigǔ hé zhēng jīdàn gēng; yǒuxiē cài wǒ cónglái méiyǒu yìnxiàng, yěxǔ shì māma xué le dàn hòulái méiyǒu zuò guò de. Wǒ pāi le měi yí yè de zhàopiàn cún zài shǒujī lǐ, ránhòu xiǎoxīn de bǎ càipǔ běn fàng huí xiāngzi. Zhège běnzi bù zhǐ shì càipǔ, tā jìlù de shì yí gè jiātíng de rìcháng, shì māma yòngxīn shēnghuó de zhèngjù.
text_en: While moving, I found my mom's recipe notebook in an old box. It was an ordinary notebook, its cover yellowed, oil stains on the corners. Opening it, inside was mom's handwriting, each dish recorded carefully. Every recipe listed the ingredients, amounts, and steps — some even had simple sketches. Some pages had little notes in the margins, like 'Dad especially loves this dish' and 'Little Bao ate two bowls of rice the first time he tried this.' Seeing 'Little Bao' made me smile — that was my childhood nickname. Some recipes I'd eaten many times, like sweet and sour ribs and steamed egg custard; some I had no memory of, maybe dishes mom learned but never ended up making. I photographed every page and saved them on my phone, then carefully put the notebook back in the box. This notebook isn't just recipes — it records a family's daily life, evidence of a mother living with heart.

questions.q_zh: 作者找到菜谱本以后做了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author do after finding the recipe notebook?

options.text_en: Took it to the new home

options.pinyin: pāi le zhàopiàn cún zài shǒujī lǐ
options.text_en: Photographed each page and saved on phone

title: Seasonal Strawberries
text_zh: 每年春天，菜市场里最让我期待的就是草莓。第一批草莓上市的时候，个头不大，颜色也不是特别红，但咬一口，那种酸甜的味道会让你马上知道：春天来了。我喜欢买回来以后不洗，先闻一闻。新鲜的草莓有一种淡淡的花香，要凑近才闻得到。洗干净以后放在白色的盘子里，红红的，很好看。我不喜欢在草莓上加糖或者做成其他东西，觉得那样会盖住它本来的味道。最好的吃法就是洗干净直接吃，一颗一颗，慢慢品尝。草莓的季节很短，大概只有一个多月。过了这段时间，虽然超市里一年四季都能买到草莓，但那些大棚种出来的，味道总是差一些。也许正是因为短暂，所以每年春天吃到第一颗草莓的时候，那种开心才特别珍贵。
text_pinyin: Měi nián chūntiān, càishìchǎng lǐ zuì ràng wǒ qīdài de jiù shì cǎoméi. Dì yī pī cǎoméi shàng shì de shíhou, gètóu bú dà, yánsè yě bú shì tèbié hóng, dàn yǎo yì kǒu, nà zhǒng suān tián de wèidào huì ràng nǐ mǎshàng zhīdào: chūntiān lái le. Wǒ xǐhuan mǎi huílái yǐhòu bù xǐ, xiān wén yì wén. Xīnxiān de cǎoméi yǒu yì zhǒng dàn dàn de huāxiāng, yào còu jìn cái wén de dào. Xǐ gānjìng yǐhòu fàng zài báisè de pánzi lǐ, hóng hóng de, hěn hǎokàn. Wǒ bù xǐhuan zài cǎoméi shàng jiā táng huòzhě zuò chéng qítā dōngxi, juéde nàyàng huì gài zhù tā běnlái de wèidào. Zuì hǎo de chīfǎ jiù shì xǐ gānjìng zhíjiē chī, yì kē yì kē, mànmàn pǐncháng. Cǎoméi de jìjié hěn duǎn, dàgài zhǐ yǒu yí gè duō yuè. Guò le zhè duàn shíjiān, suīrán chāoshì lǐ yì nián sì jì dōu néng mǎi dào cǎoméi, dàn nàxiē dàpéng zhòng chūlái de, wèidào zǒngshì chà yìxiē. Yěxǔ zhèng shì yīnwèi duǎnzàn, suǒyǐ měi nián chūntiān chī dào dì yī kē cǎoméi de shíhou, nà zhǒng kāixīn cái tèbié zhēnguì.
text_en: Every spring, what I look forward to most at the market is strawberries. When the first batch arrives, they're not very big and not particularly red, but one bite and that sweet-tart flavor tells you immediately: spring has come. I like to buy them and not wash them right away — first, I smell them. Fresh strawberries have a faint floral scent you can only catch up close. After washing, placed on a white plate, bright red, beautiful. I don't like adding sugar or turning them into something else — I think that covers their natural flavor. The best way is to wash them and eat them as they are, one by one, savoring slowly. Strawberry season is short, only about a month. After that, supermarkets carry strawberries year-round, but the greenhouse-grown ones always taste a bit off. Perhaps it's precisely because the season is fleeting that the joy of eating the first strawberry each spring feels so precious.

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得吃草莓最好的方法是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think is the best way to eat strawberries?

questions.q_zh: 草莓的季节大概有多长？
questions.q_en: How long is strawberry season approximately?

title: The Neighborhood Restaurant
text_zh: 我家楼下的街角有一家小饭馆，开了快二十年了。店面不大，装修也很普通，但每到饭点都坐满了人。老板娘是一个很爱笑的中年女人，见到每个客人都像见到老朋友一样热情。她能记住很多常客的口味，有的人一进门她就会说「今天有你爱吃的干煸四季豆」或者「给你留了一碗酸辣汤」。饭馆的菜单很简单，都是家常菜，但味道特别好。尤其是他们家的麻婆豆腐，豆腐嫩得像果冻，麻辣的味道恰到好处。我搬来这个小区以后，几乎每个星期都去吃一两次。有时候一个人去，有时候和朋友一起。一个人去的时候，老板娘会特意多给我夹一块肉，说「一个人也要吃好」。这种小饭馆在大城市越来越少了，但只要它还在，这条街就有一种家的感觉。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóu xià de jiējiǎo yǒu yì jiā xiǎo fànguǎn, kāi le kuài èrshí nián le. Diànmiàn bú dà, zhuāngxiū yě hěn pǔtōng, dàn měi dào fàndiǎn dōu zuò mǎn le rén. Lǎobǎnniáng shì yí gè hěn ài xiào de zhōngnián nǚrén, jiàn dào měi gè kèrén dōu xiàng jiàn dào lǎo péngyou yíyàng rèqíng. Tā néng jì zhù hěn duō chángkè de kǒuwèi, yǒu de rén yí jìn mén tā jiù huì shuō「jīntiān yǒu nǐ ài chī de gānbiān sìjìdòu」huòzhě「gěi nǐ liú le yì wǎn suānlà tāng」. Fànguǎn de càidān hěn jiǎndān, dōu shì jiāchángcài, dàn wèidào tèbié hǎo. Yóuqí shì tāmen jiā de mápó dòufu, dòufu nèn de xiàng guǒdòng, málà de wèidào qià dào hǎo chù. Wǒ bān lái zhège xiǎoqū yǐhòu, jīhū měi gè xīngqī dōu qù chī yí liǎng cì. Yǒu shíhou yí gè rén qù, yǒu shíhou hé péngyou yìqǐ. Yí gè rén qù de shíhou, lǎobǎnniáng huì tèyì duō gěi wǒ jiā yí kuài ròu, shuō「yí gè rén yě yào chī hǎo」. Zhè zhǒng xiǎo fànguǎn zài dà chéngshì yuè lái yuè shǎo le, dàn zhǐyào tā hái zài, zhè tiáo jiē jiù yǒu yì zhǒng jiā de gǎnjué.
text_en: At the corner below my building there's a small restaurant that's been open for nearly twenty years. The space is small, the decor ordinary, but at mealtimes every seat is taken. The owner's wife is a cheerful middle-aged woman who greets every customer like an old friend. She remembers many regulars' preferences — some people walk in and she'll say, 'Today we have your favorite dry-fried green beans,' or 'I saved you a bowl of hot and sour soup.' The menu is simple, all home-style dishes, but the flavors are exceptional. Especially their mapo tofu — the tofu as tender as jelly, the numbing-spicy flavor just right. Since moving to this neighborhood, I go almost once or twice a week. Sometimes alone, sometimes with friends. When I go alone, the owner's wife will deliberately put an extra piece of meat in my dish, saying, 'Even eating alone, you should eat well.' These kinds of small restaurants are becoming rarer in big cities, but as long as it's still here, this street has a feeling of home.

questions.q_zh: 这家饭馆开了多长时间？
questions.q_en: How long has this restaurant been open?

questions.q_zh: 作者一个人去的时候老板娘会怎么做？
questions.q_en: What does the owner's wife do when the author comes alone?

options.pinyin: duō gěi tā jiā yí kuài ròu
options.text_en: Gives him an extra piece of meat

options.pinyin: wèn tā yào bú yào diǎn wàimài

title: The Last Dumpling
text_zh: 我们家吃饭有一个习惯：盘子里最后一个饺子，谁都不好意思吃。每次过年包了很多饺子，一家人围着桌子吃得热热闹闹的，一盘接一盘地上，但到了最后总会剩下那么一两个。爸爸会说「你吃吧」，妈妈会说「给孩子吃」，我会说「我已经吃饱了」。那个饺子就在盘子里安安静静地待着，没有人动它。后来妈妈想了一个办法，她说最后一个饺子里包着好运气，谁吃了谁新的一年就会特别顺利。这样一来，大家反而开始抢了。有时候爸爸会故意让给我，说「年轻人更需要好运气」。我知道他是让着我，但还是会假装相信地开开心心吃掉。现在想起来，那个饺子里包的不是好运气，是一家人互相让来让去的温暖。那种温暖比任何馅料都珍贵。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen jiā chīfàn yǒu yí gè xíguàn: pánzi lǐ zuìhòu yí gè jiǎozi, shéi dōu bù hǎoyìsi chī. Měi cì guònián bāo le hěn duō jiǎozi, yì jiā rén wéi zhe zhuōzi chī de rè rè nào nào de, yì pán jiē yì pán de shàng, dàn dào le zuìhòu zǒng huì shèng xià nàme yí liǎng gè. Bàba huì shuō「nǐ chī ba」, māma huì shuō「gěi háizi chī」, wǒ huì shuō「wǒ yǐjīng chī bǎo le」. Nàge jiǎozi jiù zài pánzi lǐ ān ān jìng jìng de dāi zhe, méiyǒu rén dòng tā. Hòulái māma xiǎng le yí gè bànfǎ, tā shuō zuìhòu yí gè jiǎozi lǐ bāo zhe hǎo yùnqi, shéi chī le shéi xīn de yì nián jiù huì tèbié shùnlì. Zhèyàng yì lái, dàjiā fǎn'ér kāishǐ qiǎng le. Yǒu shíhou bàba huì gùyì ràng gěi wǒ, shuō「niánqīng rén gèng xūyào hǎo yùnqi」. Wǒ zhīdào tā shì ràng zhe wǒ, dàn háishi huì jiǎzhuāng xiāngxìn de kāi kāi xīn xīn chī diào. Xiànzài xiǎng qǐlái, nàge jiǎozi lǐ bāo de bú shì hǎo yùnqi, shì yì jiā rén hùxiāng ràng lái ràng qù de wēnnuǎn. Nà zhǒng wēnnuǎn bǐ rènhé xiànliào dōu zhēnguì.
text_en: Our family has a habit when eating: nobody feels comfortable taking the last dumpling on the plate. Every New Year we'd make lots of dumplings, the whole family eating lively around the table, plate after plate served, but in the end there'd always be one or two left. Dad would say 'You eat it,' Mom would say 'Give it to the child,' I'd say 'I'm already full.' That dumpling would just sit quietly on the plate, untouched. Later Mom came up with an idea — she said the last dumpling contains good luck, and whoever eats it will have a smooth new year. After that, everyone actually started competing for it. Sometimes Dad would deliberately let me have it, saying 'Young people need good luck more.' I knew he was being generous, but I'd pretend to believe it and eat it happily. Thinking about it now, what was wrapped inside that dumpling wasn't good luck — it was the warmth of a family yielding to each other back and forth. That kind of warmth is more precious than any filling.

options.text: 把最后的饺子分成几份
options.pinyin: bǎ zuìhòu de jiǎozi fēn chéng jǐ fèn
options.text_en: Split the last dumpling into pieces

options.text: 说最后一个饺子里有好运气
options.pinyin: shuō zuìhòu yí gè jiǎozi lǐ yǒu hǎo yùnqi
options.text_en: Said the last dumpling contains good luck

options.pinyin: měi cì shǎo bāo jǐ gè

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得最后一个饺子里包的是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think is wrapped in the last dumpling?

options.text: 一家人互相让来让去的温暖
options.pinyin: yì jiā rén hùxiāng ràng lái ràng qù de wēnnuǎn
options.text_en: The warmth of family yielding to each other

title: Harvest Moon Dinner
text_zh: 秋天是我最喜欢做饭的季节，因为这个季节的食材最丰富。市场上到处是金黄色的南瓜、深紫色的茄子、新鲜的板栗和各种各样的蘑菇。我喜欢买一个小南瓜回来，切开以后挖掉籽，切成小块蒸熟。蒸好的南瓜又甜又软，可以直接吃，也可以做成南瓜粥。板栗是秋天特有的味道，剥起来虽然有点麻烦，但那种甜甜的、粉粉的口感值得花时间。有时候我会做板栗烧鸡，板栗和鸡肉一起炖，味道特别好。秋天的晚上天黑得早，六点多天就暗了。在灯下做一顿暖和的晚饭，窗外的风有点凉，厨房里却是热乎乎的。把做好的菜摆在桌上，开一瓶啤酒，慢慢吃。秋天的晚餐不需要很丰盛，但一定要有季节的味道。这种跟着季节吃东西的习惯，让我觉得自己和自然还保持着一种联系。
text_pinyin: Qiūtiān shì wǒ zuì xǐhuan zuòfàn de jìjié, yīnwèi zhège jìjié de shícái zuì fēngfù. Shìchǎng shàng dàochù shì jīnhuángsè de nánguā, shēn zǐsè de qiézi, xīnxiān de bǎnlì hé gè zhǒng gè yàng de mógū. Wǒ xǐhuan mǎi yí gè xiǎo nánguā huílái, qiē kāi yǐhòu wā diào zǐ, qiē chéng xiǎo kuài zhēng shú. Zhēng hǎo de nánguā yòu tián yòu ruǎn, kěyǐ zhíjiē chī, yě kěyǐ zuò chéng nánguā zhōu. Bǎnlì shì qiūtiān tèyǒu de wèidào, bāo qǐlái suīrán yǒudiǎn máfan, dàn nà zhǒng tián tián de, fěn fěn de kǒugǎn zhídé huā shíjiān. Yǒu shíhou wǒ huì zuò bǎnlì shāo jī, bǎnlì hé jīròu yìqǐ dùn, wèidào tèbié hǎo. Qiūtiān de wǎnshang tiān hēi de zǎo, liù diǎn duō tiān jiù àn le. Zài dēng xià zuò yí dùn nuǎnhuo de wǎnfàn, chuāng wài de fēng yǒudiǎn liáng, chúfáng lǐ què shì rè hūhū de. Bǎ zuò hǎo de cài bǎi zài zhuō shàng, kāi yì píng píjiǔ, mànmàn chī. Qiūtiān de wǎncān bù xūyào hěn fēngshèng, dàn yídìng yào yǒu jìjié de wèidào. Zhè zhǒng gēn zhe jìjié chī dōngxi de xíguàn, ràng wǒ juéde zìjǐ hé zìrán hái bǎochí zhe yì zhǒng liánxì.
text_en: Autumn is my favorite season to cook, because the ingredients are richest then. The market is full of golden pumpkins, deep purple eggplants, fresh chestnuts, and all kinds of mushrooms. I like to buy a small pumpkin, cut it open, scoop out the seeds, cut into chunks and steam. Steamed pumpkin is sweet and soft — eat it directly or make pumpkin porridge. Chestnuts have a flavor unique to autumn; peeling them is a bit tedious, but that sweet, starchy taste is worth the time. Sometimes I make braised chicken with chestnuts — stewed together, the flavor is wonderful. Autumn evenings get dark early, past six and it's already dim. Under the light, making a warm dinner, the wind outside a bit cool, the kitchen warm and cozy. Arrange the finished dishes on the table, open a bottle of beer, eat slowly. An autumn dinner doesn't need to be elaborate, but it must have the taste of the season. This habit of eating with the seasons makes me feel I still maintain a connection with nature.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么最喜欢秋天做饭？
questions.q_en: Why does the author most enjoy cooking in autumn?

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得秋天的晚餐最重要的是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think is most important for an autumn dinner?

options.text_en: It must have the taste of the season

options.pinyin: yào huā hěn cháng shíjiān zuò
options.text_en: It should take a long time to make

title: Sharing Food Across Cultures
text_zh: 我的邻居是一对日本夫妻，他们搬来的时候送了我一盒日式点心。我尝了以后觉得很好吃，就做了一盘红烧茄子送过去作为回礼。没想到他们特别喜欢，第二天就来敲门问我怎么做的。从那以后我们经常交换食物。他们教我做日式咖喱饭和味噌汤，我教他们做番茄炒蛋和凉拌黄瓜。有意思的是，同样一个食材，不同文化的做法完全不同。比如豆腐，中国人喜欢炒着吃或者炖着吃，日本人喜欢凉着吃，放上葱花和酱油。两种吃法都好吃，但是感觉完全不一样。有一次我们决定一起做一顿中日混合的晚餐。我做了几道中国菜，他们做了几道日本菜，摆满了整张桌子。大家一边吃一边用简单的英语和手势交流。语言不通也没关系，因为好吃的表情是全世界通用的。那顿饭让我明白，食物是最好的沟通方式之一。
text_pinyin: Wǒ de línjū shì yí duì Rìběn fūqī, tāmen bān lái de shíhou sòng le wǒ yì hé Rìshì diǎnxīn. Wǒ cháng le yǐhòu juéde hěn hǎochī, jiù zuò le yì pán hóngshāo qiézi sòng guòqù zuòwéi huílǐ. Méi xiǎng dào tāmen tèbié xǐhuan, dì èr tiān jiù lái qiāo mén wèn wǒ zěnme zuò de. Cóng nà yǐhòu wǒmen jīngcháng jiāohuàn shíwù. Tāmen jiāo wǒ zuò Rìshì gālí fàn hé wèizēng tāng, wǒ jiāo tāmen zuò fānqié chǎo dàn hé liángbàn huángguā. Yǒu yìsi de shì, tóngyàng yí gè shícái, bùtóng wénhuà de zuòfǎ wánquán bùtóng. Bǐrú dòufu, Zhōngguó rén xǐhuan chǎo zhe chī huòzhě dùn zhe chī, Rìběn rén xǐhuan liáng zhe chī, fàng shàng cōnghuā hé jiàngyóu. Liǎng zhǒng chīfǎ dōu hǎochī, dànshì gǎnjué wánquán bù yíyàng. Yǒu yí cì wǒmen juédìng yìqǐ zuò yí dùn Zhōng Rì hùnhé de wǎncān. Wǒ zuò le jǐ dào Zhōngguó cài, tāmen zuò le jǐ dào Rìběn cài, bǎi mǎn le zhěng zhāng zhuōzi. Dàjiā yìbiān chī yìbiān yòng jiǎndān de Yīngyǔ hé shǒushì jiāoliú. Yǔyán bù tōng yě méi guānxi, yīnwèi hǎochī de biǎoqíng shì quán shìjiè tōngyòng de. Nà dùn fàn ràng wǒ míngbai, shíwù shì zuì hǎo de gōutōng fāngshì zhī yī.
text_en: My neighbors are a Japanese couple. When they moved in, they gave me a box of Japanese sweets. I thought they were delicious and sent over a plate of braised eggplant in return. To my surprise they loved it, and the next day knocked on my door asking how to make it. Since then we often exchange food. They taught me Japanese curry rice and miso soup; I taught them scrambled eggs with tomato and cold cucumber salad. The interesting thing is, the same ingredient is prepared completely differently across cultures. Take tofu — Chinese people like to stir-fry or braise it, Japanese people like to eat it cold with scallions and soy sauce. Both ways are delicious, but the feeling is entirely different. Once we decided to make a Chinese-Japanese fusion dinner together. I made several Chinese dishes, they made several Japanese ones, filling the entire table. Everyone ate while communicating in simple English and gestures. Not speaking the same language didn't matter, because the expression for 'delicious' is universal. That meal taught me that food is one of the best forms of communication.

questions.q_zh: 邻居搬来的时候送了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the neighbors bring when they moved in?
What does the author think food is?

options.pinyin: zuì hǎo de gōutōng fāngshì zhī yī
options.text_en: One of the best forms of communication

title: The Comfort of Congee
text_zh: 生病的时候最想吃的就是一碗白粥。不是那种加了很多东西的粥，就是最普通的白米粥，熬得稠稠的，米粒已经几乎化开了。小时候每次发烧，妈妈都会熬一锅白粥，盛在一个小碗里，旁边放一碟子咸菜。她会坐在床边，用勺子一口一口地喂我。那时候觉得白粥没什么味道，不如肉和菜好吃。但生病的时候胃口不好，别的东西都吃不下，只有白粥可以一口一口慢慢咽下去。温温的，软软的，到了肚子里很舒服。现在我自己生病了也会熬粥。把米洗好放进锅里，加很多水，开大火煮开以后转小火，偶尔搅一搅，防止粘锅。大概四十分钟以后，粥就熬好了。一个人坐在桌前喝着粥，虽然没有妈妈坐在旁边，但那种被温暖包围的感觉，还是一样的。也许这就是食物的力量——在你最脆弱的时候，一碗简单的粥就能给你力量。
text_pinyin: Shēngbìng de shíhou zuì xiǎng chī de jiù shì yì wǎn bái zhōu. Bú shì nà zhǒng jiā le hěn duō dōngxi de zhōu, jiù shì zuì pǔtōng de bái mǐ zhōu, áo de chóu chóu de, mǐ lì yǐjīng jīhū huà kāi le. Xiǎo shíhou měi cì fāshāo, māma dōu huì áo yì guō bái zhōu, chéng zài yí gè xiǎo wǎn lǐ, pángbiān fàng yí diézi xiáncài. Tā huì zuò zài chuáng biān, yòng sháozi yì kǒu yì kǒu de wèi wǒ. Nà shíhou juéde bái zhōu méi shénme wèidào, bù rú ròu hé cài hǎochī. Dàn shēngbìng de shíhou wèikǒu bù hǎo, bié de dōngxi dōu chī bú xià, zhǐ yǒu bái zhōu kěyǐ yì kǒu yì kǒu mànmàn yàn xiàqù. Wēn wēn de, ruǎn ruǎn de, dào le dùzi lǐ hěn shūfu. Xiànzài wǒ zìjǐ shēngbìng le yě huì áo zhōu. Bǎ mǐ xǐ hǎo fàng jìn guō lǐ, jiā hěn duō shuǐ, kāi dà huǒ zhǔ kāi yǐhòu zhuǎn xiǎo huǒ, ǒu'ěr jiǎo yì jiǎo, fángzhǐ zhān guō. Dàgài sìshí fēnzhōng yǐhòu, zhōu jiù áo hǎo le. Yí gè rén zuò zài zhuō qián hē zhe zhōu, suīrán méiyǒu māma zuò zài pángbiān, dàn nà zhǒng bèi wēnnuǎn bāowéi de gǎnjué, háishi yíyàng de. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì shíwù de lìliàng — zài nǐ zuì cuìruò de shíhou, yì wǎn jiǎndān de zhōu jiù néng gěi nǐ lìliàng.
text_en: When sick, what I most want to eat is a bowl of plain congee. Not the kind loaded with things, just the most ordinary white rice porridge, simmered thick until the grains have nearly dissolved. As a child, every time I had a fever, Mom would make a pot of plain congee, served in a small bowl with a dish of pickled vegetables on the side. She'd sit at the bedside, feeding me spoonful by spoonful. Back then I thought congee had no flavor, not as good as meat and vegetables. But when sick, with no appetite, nothing else goes down — only congee can be swallowed slowly, mouthful by mouthful. Warm, soft, comfortable in the stomach. Now when I'm sick, I also make congee. Wash the rice, put it in the pot, add lots of water, bring to a boil on high then turn to low, stir occasionally to prevent sticking. About forty minutes later, the congee is done. Sitting alone at the table drinking congee, even without Mom beside me, that feeling of being wrapped in warmth is still the same. Perhaps this is the power of food — at your most vulnerable, a simple bowl of congee can give you strength.

questions.q_zh: 小时候妈妈熬粥的时候旁边放什么？
questions.q_en: What did mom put beside the congee when making it in childhood?

questions.q_zh: 熬粥大概需要多长时间？
questions.q_en: How long does it take to make congee?

title: Morning Buns at the Corner Shop
text_zh: 每天早上六点半，街口的包子铺就开始冒蒸汽了。还没走近就能闻到面粉和肉的香味，那种味道在清晨的冷空气里特别好闻。包子铺很小，只有一个窗口，里面是两个忙碌的人——老板和他的妻子。老板负责包，他的手特别快，一个包子只需要几秒钟就包好了。妻子负责蒸和卖，她会把蒸好的包子一笼一笼地摆在窗口，热气从竹蒸笼里往上飘。他们家的包子有三种馅：猪肉大葱、韭菜鸡蛋和豆沙的。我最喜欢猪肉大葱的，皮薄馅多，咬一口会流出一点汤汁。每天早上买两个包子加一杯豆浆，站在路边吃完，然后去上班。这个小小的仪式感让每一天的开始都有了温度。有时候排队的人多，大家在寒风里站着等，但没有人抱怨。因为大家都知道，等一等是值得的。好的早餐，值得你早起五分钟。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang liù diǎn bàn, jiēkǒu de bāozi pù jiù kāishǐ mào zhēngqì le. Hái méi zǒu jìn jiù néng wén dào miànfěn hé ròu de xiāngwèi, nà zhǒng wèidào zài qīngchén de lěng kōngqì lǐ tèbié hǎo wén. Bāozi pù hěn xiǎo, zhǐ yǒu yí gè chuāngkǒu, lǐmiàn shì liǎng gè mánglù de rén — lǎobǎn hé tā de qīzi. Lǎobǎn fùzé bāo, tā de shǒu tèbié kuài, yí gè bāozi zhǐ xūyào jǐ miǎozhōng jiù bāo hǎo le. Qīzi fùzé zhēng hé mài, tā huì bǎ zhēng hǎo de bāozi yì lóng yì lóng de bǎi zài chuāngkǒu, rèqì cóng zhú zhēnglóng lǐ wǎng shàng piāo. Tāmen jiā de bāozi yǒu sān zhǒng xiàn: zhūròu dàcōng, jiǔcài jīdàn hé dòushā de. Wǒ zuì xǐhuan zhūròu dàcōng de, pí báo xiàn duō, yǎo yì kǒu huì liú chū yìdiǎn tāngzhī. Měi tiān zǎoshang mǎi liǎng gè bāozi jiā yì bēi dòujiāng, zhàn zài lù biān chī wán, ránhòu qù shàngbān. Zhège xiǎo xiǎo de yíshì gǎn ràng měi yì tiān de kāishǐ dōu yǒu le wēndù. Yǒu shíhou páiduì de rén duō, dàjiā zài hánfēng lǐ zhàn zhe děng, dàn méiyǒu rén bàoyuàn. Yīnwèi dàjiā dōu zhīdào, děng yì děng shì zhídé de. Hǎo de zǎocān, zhídé nǐ zǎo qǐ wǔ fēnzhōng.
text_en: Every morning at 6:30, the bun shop on the corner starts steaming. Before you even get close you can smell the flour and meat — that scent is especially wonderful in the cold morning air. The shop is tiny, just one window, behind it two busy people — the owner and his wife. He's in charge of wrapping, his hands incredibly fast, each bun done in just seconds. His wife handles steaming and selling, placing finished buns basket by basket at the window, steam rising from the bamboo steamers. They have three fillings: pork and scallion, chive and egg, and red bean paste. My favorite is pork and scallion — thin skin, generous filling, a bit of broth flows out when you bite. Every morning I buy two buns and a cup of soy milk, eat standing by the road, then head to work. This small ritual gives every day's beginning a warmth. Sometimes the line is long, everyone standing in the cold wind waiting, but nobody complains. Because everyone knows the wait is worth it. A good breakfast is worth getting up five minutes early.

questions.q_en: How many fillings does the bun shop offer?
What does the author buy every morning?

options.pinyin: yí gè bāozi hé yì wǎn zhōu
options.text_en: One bun and a bowl of congee

options.pinyin: liǎng gè bāozi hé yì bēi dòujiāng
options.text_en: Two buns and a cup of soy milk

options.pinyin: sān gè bāozi hé yì bēi chá
options.text_en: Three buns and a cup of tea

options.pinyin: yí gè jiānbing hé yì bēi niúnǎi
options.text_en: One crepe and a glass of milk
